title: Stroopwafels
subtitle: Dutch syrup waffles, sandwiched with caramel
excerpt: Two thin waffle discs baked in a waffle iron, sandwiched together while hot with a soft caramel syrup. The waffle sets crisp once cool, but the caramel stays soft. Traditionally placed over a cup of hot coffee to warm and soften.
sourceNotes: Stroopwafels originate in Gouda in the early 19th century, invented by bakery owner Gerard Kamphuisen around 1810. Pre-1928 Dutch household guides and period market records document the biscuit. The filling is a thin caramel syrup (stroop) made from butter, brown sugar, and cinnamon. The biscuit became a Dutch national food icon and is now produced and sold worldwide.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stroopwafels keep in an airtight tin for 1 week. The caramel may harden slightly; a few minutes over a warm cup will soften it.

glossaryTerms.definition: Stroopwafels are made by splitting a warm baked waffle disc horizontally through the middle using a sharp knife, then spreading caramel on one face and pressing the two halves back together. The waffle must be split while warm and pliable; a cool waffle is too brittle to cut without crumbling.

content.text: Stroopwafels require a waffle iron and a willingness to work fast. The waffle must be split while warm, caramel spread while hot, and pressed shut immediately. The
is the most demanding part: a serrated knife and confidence.

Combine the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar. Add the melted butter, egg, and warm milk and mix to a soft dough. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Cover and leave for 30 minutes to rise slightly; the dough will not double but should feel noticeably puffed.

Melt the butter in a saucepan, add the brown sugar, golden syrup, and cinnamon. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves and the mixture is smooth and thick. Keep warm over very low heat while you bake the waffles.

Preheat a round waffle iron. Divide the dough into 36 balls of about 20 g each. Roll each ball into a round slightly smaller than the waffle iron. Cook for 1 to 2 minutes per waffle until golden brown. Work immediately: split each hot waffle through the middle horizontally with a sharp knife, spread one side with a generous teaspoon of caramel syrup, and press the two halves together firmly. Set aside on a rack to cool. Work quickly; waffles become brittle within 2 minutes of coming off the iron.
